item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was already pretty on board to start with, but the thing that got my upvote was that aside from asking for a bunch of really sketchy stuff and dire warnings to the contrary, this skip doesn't actually do anything terrible to anyone! :D Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this, and feel that the items the scavenger hunt requests are very creative and, as someone else said, very WTF. It makes me wish that there were more tests conducted on the item so I could enjoy more wtf-ish weirdness, but I also think it's perfect as-is. Also, I read through all the comments here and it's really great to see how receptive you were to feedback and how hard you worked to make this excellent.

+1 For sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like most of the items on the list would be more likely to be found through assault & battery or murder, but I guess if they're robbing hospitals and morgues and shit like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good things come to those who don't wait to make necessary edits!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda funny to see this featured, seeing just how much time and effort was put into to keep this from being deleted.

SCP-1431 was recovered from a house in ████████, Canada in an active state after reports of ‘puzzling' robberies had been circulating the town's local news.

and holy shit this fuckin line here

item.title: The last paragraph of the second letter
item.content:encoded: Haha I'm not dumb and totally didn't edit this from something dumb I posted yesterday

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We found a d-class who happened to have some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a necro post, sorry. I just want to know which emplyee provided a hard drive with porn of a relative? I mean, really? Or did I misread that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, especially for a first SCP. The 'DATA EXPUNGED' was what made me hit +.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just now showed up and didn't have a chance to see how mediocre past versions were, and I can honestly say this does a decent job. Loved the "Data redacted" one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Previously, I hadn't voted - I was waiting to see if you improved it enough. And you did! Well done. Have this upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are a gentleman and a scholar :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had already changed the 'always being "normal" phrase' to items being "easily accessible" to subjects, perhaps you didn't see it. I'll fix the "motivational" part. The only other subjective thing I could think of was within the recovery of the object (description) it mentions that "puzzling" robberies were reported. The quotes are written in to show that this is how the town's news reports described it. If you can find any other subjective phrases, do feel free to let me know.

As for your issue with the description, I don't think I understand you correctly. Are you saying you want me to include everything else that is on the sheet prior to the list manifesting? If so, I didn't feel that was necessary as 'The title of the game and the rules of a standard scavenger hunt' seem adequately self explanatory and are the only things on the sheet (which is indicated). What are you referring to when you say the description is missing the actual text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of tonal issues. It doesn't read like a scientific document, it reads like an approximation of one.

There are several instances where the report makes observations or value judgements that wouldn't or couldn't be made by a researcher. Two examples are the characterization of the note at the end as "motivational" and the reference to one of the items always being "normal".

Aside from tone, the biggest problem I have with this piece is that the description gives the content of the note instead of containing the actual text.

All together, it comes across as a detailed write-up of an idea for an SCP article rather than an article in it's own right.

I do love the list, though. It's simple and elegantly creepy in a matter-of-fact way and I dig that.

No vote for now but I'd love to upvote if it can be made to read more like a technical document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-sniff- I supported you from the beginning! I knew you could do it!

The writing is REALLY professional and EVERYTHING is just spot-on now. I like each and every item, I like the "prize." I checked the history to see who edited it for you, but it was all you. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Site numbers aren't canon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all thank you for taking the time to write out such a detailed response!

I'm going to touch on some things you said that I don't agree with, but you also have a lot valid points and I have changed things accordingly. :)

you never state anywhere what happens when someone wins or loses.

When someone wins they receive a gift basket, I have added in a few lines that explain that nothing happens to either subject upon completion of the game if they both participated (besides the gift basket). I feel that the fact that they are forced to do this all for nothing makes it a little bit darker and that's what I was aiming for. I've added in a couple of lines explaining the legitimacy of the 'motivational note' as well.

I have no idea what any of that means.

I feel like you 'not having any idea of what it means' is not the result of my writing unclearly as much as it is you not feeling that 'easily accessible items' are interesting. I somewhat agree, and I've changed two of the easily accessible items. However, I am keeping one at the end of every test for comic relief.

And then you go and ruin everything. The Detonator of A B61 Nuclear Warhead. I don't give a shit about that. Other "normal" objects, like a Fountain Pen, I get the idea behind putting them on the list, but they don't work either. I don't want ordinary or dangerous or expensive or hard to find. I want more wtfy Salvadorian weirdness.

I agree here so I've been brainstorming and changed some of the items accordingly.

Also your quote symbols jump from “ to " and back again. Pick one.

fixed

Again you don't reveal what happened to either subject. If nothing happens, what's the point of collecting this stuff?

The idea of SCP-1431 is that you end up being forced to gather all these ridiculous terrible things, ultimately traumatizing you for nothing more than a crappy door prize.

Changes made according to all the things you mentioned that I agreed with. Let me know how you feel or if there is something else you would like to add!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is highly disappointing, because it could've been really great, but isn't.

Below the rules is a small motivational note declaring, "Failure to attempt to gather items is poor sportsmanship and will result in feelings of despair, followed by death. Better get cracking!”

You never state whether this is just a note, or an actual anomalous effect. Actually, you never state anywhere what happens when someone wins or looses.

However, a certain uniform theme in the designated items is distinctly present between each test. Field tests indicate that there is always at least one designated 'normal' item on the list.

I have no idea what any of that means.

Observable scavenger hunt list upon recovery of SCP-1431

I love the weird surreal format of "[Object] which [weird trait]". A Gift Given Out of Guilt. The Fingerprint of A Finger Without A Nail. These are great. I mean, wtf, a finger has the same fingerprint with or without a nail. But it only counts if it's a fingerprint of a finger without a nail. WTF. This is great. This works.

And then you go and ruin everything. The Detonator of A B61 Nuclear Warhead. I don't give a shit about that. Other "normal" objects, like a Fountain Pen, I get the idea behind putting them on the list, but they don't work either. I don't want ordinary or dangerous or expensive or hard to find. I want more wtfy Salvadorian weirdness.

Some other entries are on the right track but don't quite hit it. "A Computer Containing Candid Photography of Someone Dear to You" is not weird at all and also overcomplicated when you can just specify a photograph. "A Notepad That Caught The Blood Spilled By A Suicide" is again overcomplicated when you can just specify blood, and also too close to the previous "Blood Spilt Through Envy", which was good. “An Eye Willingly Gouged Out By its Owner” and “A Knife Recently Jammed Into An Electricity Socket” are too gory and not enough weird. [DATA REDACTED] is a nice twist on redaction, but doesn't quite work in the context of a scavenger hunt.

Also your quote symbols jump from “ to " and back again. Pick one.

██ days, 6 hours and 58 minutes were spent recovering all the items on the list. Subject-A was provided with all of the designated items on the list and Subject-B was given nothing.

Again you don't reveal what happened to either subject. If nothing happens, what's the point of collecting this stuff?

Everything below the additional notes I don't care about. It isn't bad, but if it wasn't there it wouldn't change anything for me.

My downvote stays for now, but I would be very happy to see this further improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your support from the beginning either way! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this really turned things around! Glad to see this taking off! Nice job, flower!
I kind of wish i hadn't already +1'd, so i could upvote the new version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have the immense pleasure of casting the vote to put this into the double digits. Congratulations; this is a incredibly solid first article and I hope to see more from you. Downvote changed to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made me laugh lol

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much :D:D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So much discussion going on here!

That last line got a chuckle out of me, and this reminds me of my first SCP. Congrats, you changed my downvote to an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Below the rules is a small motivational note declaring, "Failure to attempt to gather items is poor sportsmanship and will result in feelings of despair, followed by death. Better get cracking!”

"motivational"…

HOLY SHIT
I wish I can upvote this again, simply due to that line!
EDIT:I just read the rest of this and I must say, this is a great article! Well done!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: DrBright upvoted my SCP. Whether it gets deleted or not, I can die happy

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed to an upvote.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding for deletion. I'd like to see this again, with some changes.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Starting deletion vote at -10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There was variety and the several admins told me to put less variety and make it more one theme, so I did that. Can't please everyone,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed and yes you can use it in a story

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really enjoyed this. Mind if I use this in a story?
Also, on a side note, you might want to change the site from site-33 because of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The big problem with this scp is that there isn't much variety in terms of what the notes ask for. It feels more like "Salvador's Spoooky Scavenger Hunt" than strange.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't say this was fixed. I think you just added more items without giving any thought to an underlying cohesive narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just was advising him to take his time starting off; something I've seen said frequently. I apologize UglyFlower if it came off as judgemental; the concept itself is solid, it just has a few tonal issues in my opinion.

E: I also make no claims that my writing would be any better at this point, thus my lurking. Again, apologies to both of you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kye: Considering you've only been here a week… Matthew 7 1-5 may hold some relevance to the discussion.

UglyFlower: I'm rather in agreement with Kalinin and FortuneFavorsBold, but you seem to be listening to people here. Listening is always a good thing, in my mind.

And sometimes having an idea before you joined gets you really motivated to join, but still it can be a very bad idea. My first idea was someone similar to journal that wrote an alternate version of the author, and I was told quite quickly that the idea had been done in literature before (You'd think I hadn't read Terry Pratchett…) and not to give it a try. Upon reflection, I agreed, and moved onto lurking, then posting a much more successful SCP.

Good luck!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Potential is good, but just seems underdeveloped. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, but I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am currently working on it and doing exactly that! Thank you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my personal opinion, this is pretty much a joke with no punchline. What the article needs is a reason for people to care about it. Once again, I think you can play with what would happen in case someone actually finish the game. Does the paper give you eternal youth or there are some "optional prizes"? That simple question can open an unlimited quantity of doors. You can do whatever you want with your article to make it something novel and cool1.

Footnotes
1. Of course while following the rules and the site's high standards..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Go to the forums and ask for suggestions. There are plenty of people with good suggestions there
Specifically, This should be helpful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The current version has had a lot of work done on it post them telling me to add a lot of work. The most common criticism is, "It is a perfectly decent article that doesn't quite make it to 'interesting'". Because I have no idea what I could possibly do to make it 'interesting' other than attach laser beams to it. I'm probably going to scrap it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[DATA REDACTED]

I laugh a lot because of this (in the good way). Still, as people say, it needs a lot of work. Polish it, get some feedback and it would be great in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Idea is nice, but it seems like a combination of SCP-1883 (Gamification, which has the "points" aspect, and the searching to a degree; the example task in the picture involves finding a horse) and SCP-1590 (The Book of Tamlin, which is a program for touch screen hardware and involves finding things such as "the graves of all seven of your childhood pets your parents told you ran away.")

While the novelty of someone actually needing to find physical items is interesting, I can't help but feel that it's not unique enough to stand out from the existing SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've refined it quite a bit if you'd care to take a gander :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Containment Procedures are short. They don't need to be long, but I think they're a little too short.

This isn't good feedback. Why are they too short? They can't simply be too short. They need to be sufficient. And that's all.

However, I will say that being locked in a file cabinet sounds really insufficient. At minimum it should be in a "containment locker".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I've added in the oomph you might be looking for :) let me know!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lengthened the procedure, but not by much. Also looked for any grammar mistakes and corrected a few things. If you notice any others that I've missed, do feel free to let me know! :) Thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noted and fixed! :) Good Call, I've completely changed the tone to items that are just weird as hell along with being [as you said] "Sprinkled in with some mundane ones". Cheers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely coincidence. I've also now completely changed the list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Flower, did you intentionally reference a handful of SCPs in those lists? Or is it just a coincidence/I'm losing my mind?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm definitely up for it! Always welcome to any criticism :) Let me know if you see me and I will vice versa!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, dude, this has so much potential. It's not using much of it now, but I can tell that it's there. I'd love to bang this out with you on chat, if you'd like. Don't think it's gonna survive in this iteration, but I look forward to the rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The most obvious way to give this the evocative/memorable content it needs would be to, first, pick a tone for the scavenger hunt. Right now it has some creepy elements and some that are keyed in to the thoughts of the user (though those might be the same) sprinkled in with some mundane ones, and variety is good, but we can't really read much into what's going on, or feel much about it. Is the process alien? Malicious? Wonderous? Mischievous? There are lots of things it could be doing that it isn't.

Alternatively, if you don't want to ascribe any sort of hidden agency or purpose to the SCP like that, you could make the circumstances more interesting, based on who has been using it, how long, why, and so on.

To combine the two, maybe the scavenger hunt is a little more complex than a list of everyday objects (and a simple task)? It could be specific objects that are hard to find, tasks you wouldn't expect from a scavenger hunt ("Get a job at a shrimp research station and remain employed there for two months"), and that affects whoever has been running around checking these things in surprising ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Containment Procedures are short. They don't need to be long, but I think they're a little too short.
Other than that and a few easily fixable grammatical errors, I think the skip is fine. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you sir! :)
